title: Topology of pulsating active matter: Defect asymmetry controls emergent motility
authors: Luca Casagrande, Alessandro Manacorda, Etienne Fodor
abstract: In pulsating active matter, topological defects are motile despite the absence of any macroscopic flows and microscopic self-propulsion. We reveal that this motility arises from a ratchet effect: the mechanochemical coupling between local oscillations and repulsive interactions breaks both spatial and time-reversal symmetries, thus leading asymmetric rotating defects to drift under fluctuations. This mechanism regulates a crossover between spiral waves connecting slow defects and fiber-like waves connecting fast defects, in analogy with the onset of heart rhythm disorder in cardiac tissues. We rationalize this crossover in terms of a fluctuating hydrodynamics that captures how motile defects spontaneously nucleate and move within an ordered background.
summary: This paper explains how topological defects in pulsating active matter can move without macroscopic flows through a symmetry-breaking ratchet effect. It demonstrates that the asymmetry of defect cores controls a transition between different wave patterns, similar to cardiac arrhythmias. The work provides a hydrodynamic framework to predict these emergent motile behaviors.
main_problem: Understanding the mechanism behind the motility of topological defects in pulsating active matter in the absence of macroscopic flows or microscopic self-propulsion.
main_result: The authors reveal that defect motility arises from a ratchet effect caused by mechanochemical coupling that breaks spatial and time-reversal symmetries, leading to a crossover between spiral and fiber-like wave patterns.
method: The study uses a combination of overdamped Langevin dynamics for microscopic particles and a coarse-grained fluctuating hydrodynamics framework with a specific closure scheme for angular moments.
model_or_system: A system of pulsating active particles in a periodic box where particle sizes oscillate periodically based on an internal chemical phase, featuring repulsive interactions that break symmetries.
key_observables: Defect velocity distribution, mean-squared displacement of defect cores, asymmetry factor of the phase profile, and phase patterns (spiral vs. fiber-like waves).
important_parameters: Particle density (rho), synchronization strength (epsilon), pulsation frequency (omega), amplitude of deformation (lambda), and asymmetry factor (chi).
main_assumptions: The hydrodynamic theory assumes a constant density (discarding position-based interactions), uses an ansatz for the probability distribution to close the moment hierarchy, and approximates repulsion as an external field.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows deformation waves and asymmetric cores; Figure 2 illustrates the transition from spiral to fiber-like waves and defect displacement; Figure 3 presents the phase diagram and analytical defect profiles.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the microscopic model of pulsating particles, develops a hydrodynamic theory via coarse-graining and closure schemes, analyzes the ratchet mechanism and defect asymmetry, and concludes with a discussion on pattern crossovers and biological analogies.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The rotation is set by defect charges +1 (◦, CW) and −1 (□, CCW), while profile asymmetry is similar for ±1 defects. Parameters in Ref. [52].
